Hearing Re: PLAINTIFF KEN ELDER's Motion For Approval Of (1) Settlement Agreement And Dismissal Of Action; And (2) Relator's Application For Relator's Fee.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff and qui tam relator Ken Elder seeks an order approving a settlement agreement and dismissing this California False Claims Act (CFCA) action. The motion is unopposed and Elder's counsel provides the settlement agreement with defendant U.S. Bank N.A. and declares under penalty of perjury that the California Attorney General's Office consents to the settlement agreement and dismissal. The motion attaches as Exhibit C to the Golub declaration a written consent to dismissal signed by that office.

For good cause shown in the moving papers, "taking into account the best interests of the parties involved and the public purposes behind" the CFCA (Cal. Gov. Code 12652(c)(1)), the court grants the motion. The court further finds the relator's fee of $6340.60 to be reasonable pursuant to Government Code 12652(g)(3).

The action is dismissed.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling, with a cover letter reflecting compliance with CRC 3.1312.

RELATOR'S APPLICATION FOR LEAVE TO FILE THIRD AMENDED COMPLAINT (TENTATIVE RULING PART 2 OF 2)
Off Calendar Jun-13-2023 Off Calendar

RELATOR'S APPLICATION FOR LEAVE TO FILE THIRD AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-13-2023 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. DAVID S. GOLUB, Esq. of Silver Golub & Teitell LLP (Tel: 203-325-4491) for Relator;
Atty. KASEY J. CURTIS, Esq. of Reed Smith LLP (Tel: 213-457-8000) for defendant U.S. Bank, N.A.

HEARING RE: RELATOR'S APPLICATION FOR LEAVE TO FILE THIRD AMENDED COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's "Application for Leave to File Third Amended Complaint" is granted. The motion is timely, there is a liberal policy in favor of amendment, and the proposed amendment does not prejudice defendant since there is no trial date and the amendment does not change the tenor of the case.

Judicial estoppel does not prevent plaintiff from seeking to plead a claim under sec. 1510(b)(1) of the Unclaimed Property Law (CCP 1500 et seq. ("UPL").)

The judicial estoppel "doctrine applies when '(1) the same party has taken two positions; (2) the positions were taken in judicial or quasi-judicial administrative proceedings; (3) the party was successful in asserting the first position (i.e., the tribunal adopted the position or accepted it as true); (4) the two positions are totally inconsistent; and (5) the first position was not taken as a result of ignorance, fraud, or mistake.'" (Aguilar v. Lerner (2004) 32 Cal.4th 974, 986-87.) Judicial estoppel is predicated on the principle that a party should not be allowed to "gain[] advantage by taking one position, and then seeking a second advantage by taking an incompatible position." (Aguilar v. Lerner (2004) 32 Cal.4th 974, 986.)

Here, plaintiff's notice of dismissal of the UPL sec. 1511 claim was not a promise or representation that it would not attempt to pursue a UPL sec. 1510(b)(1) claim. Plaintiff's dismissal did not impact the issues presented in defendant's petition before the California Supreme Court, i.e., whether plaintiff stated a claim or whether Ohio was a necessary party. (Curtis Decl., Exs. D, F.) The court therefore cannot conclude that plaintiff took two inconsistent positions or that the California Supreme Court adopted plaintiff's first position as a basis to dismiss the petition for review.

Defendant argues that the UPL sec. 1510(b)(1) claim fails as a matter of law. Defendant may raise its merits arguments by subsequent motion. (Kittredge Sports Co. v. Superior Court (1989) 213 Cal.App.3d 1045, 1048 ["[T]he preferable practice would be to permit the amendment and allow the parties to test its legal sufficiency"].)

Plaintiff must file a Third Amended Complaint. The court does not deem pleadings filed.

Order signed.
